back: The management of the activities that create goods and services. It focuses on turning inputs like labor, materials, equipment, and information into outputs customers value.
Physical products that customers can see, touch, store, and transport. Examples include cars, phones, food, and furniture.
Activities or experiences provided to customers rather than physical products. Examples include banking, healthcare, consulting, transportation, and education.
The process of converting inputs into outputs. In business, this means using resources to create goods or services customers want.
The resources used to produce a good or service. Common inputs include labor, materials, machines, money, information, and time.
The goods or services produced by an operation. Outputs are what the customer receives or uses.
Work that increases the usefulness or worth of a product or service from the customer's point of view. Managers try to increase value-added work and reduce waste.
A measure of how efficiently inputs are turned into outputs. Productivity = Output ÷ Input.
Productivity measured using only one type of input, such as labor hours or machine hours. Single-Factor Productivity = Output ÷ One Input.
Productivity measured using more than one input, such as labor, materials, and overhead together. Multifactor Productivity = Output ÷ Combined Inputs.
How well resources are used with minimal waste. Efficiency often compares actual output to expected or standard output.
How well an operation achieves the right goals or customer outcomes. A process can be efficient but not effective if it does the wrong thing quickly.
The plan for how operations will support the company's overall business strategy. It decides what the operation should be especially good at, such as low cost, speed, quality, or flexibility.
The performance areas a company uses to compete in the market. Common priorities include cost, quality, speed, flexibility, and reliability.
The money spent to produce and deliver a good or service. Competing on cost means designing operations to keep expenses low while still meeting customer needs.
How well a product or service meets customer expectations. Quality can mean durability, accuracy, appearance, performance, or consistency.
How quickly a company can produce, deliver, or respond to customers. Faster operations can reduce waiting time and improve customer satisfaction.
The ability of an operation to change what it makes, how much it makes, or how quickly it adapts. Flexible operations can respond better to changing customer needs.
The ability to deliver what was promised dependably and consistently. In operations, reliability often means meeting due dates, specifications, and service commitments.
A factor that causes customers to choose one company's product or service over competitors. For example, the lowest price or fastest delivery may be the order winner.
A minimum requirement a company must meet just to be considered by customers. Order qualifiers do not usually win the sale, but failing them can eliminate the company from consideration.
A production approach where goods are made before customers place specific orders. It works best when demand is predictable and customers expect fast availability.
A production approach where work begins only after a customer places an order. It allows customization but usually creates longer wait times.
A production approach where standard parts are made ahead of time and assembled after a customer order arrives. It balances speed with some customization.
The ability to produce customized products or services at a scale and cost closer to mass production. It often relies on modular design, flexible processes, and strong information systems.
An operations approach that considers people, planet, and profit. Managers evaluate performance using social, environmental, and financial results.
A set of steps that turns inputs into outputs. In operations, processes show how work actually gets done.
A visual picture of the steps, decisions, and flows in a process. It helps managers see delays, handoffs, bottlenecks, and waste.
A diagram that shows the sequence of steps in a process using symbols and arrows. It is commonly used to analyze how work moves from start to finish.
One activity or task within a larger process. Each step should be examined to see whether it adds value, creates delay, or causes errors.
A place in a process where the next step depends on a condition or choice. Decision points often appear as yes-or-no branches in a flowchart.
The transfer of work, information, or responsibility from one person, team, or system to another. Handoffs can create delays, confusion, or errors if not managed well.
The time between completing one unit and completing the next unit. Cycle Time = Available Production Time ÷ Units Produced.
The total time it takes for one unit to move through the entire process from start to finish. It includes processing time, waiting time, movement, inspection, and delays.
The number of units a process completes in a given period of time. Throughput Rate = Units Completed ÷ Time.
The maximum output a process, machine, worker, or facility can produce over a period of time. Capacity helps managers know whether demand can be met.
The maximum output a system could produce under ideal conditions. It is usually higher than what the operation can realistically produce every day.
The realistic maximum output after considering normal limits like maintenance, staffing, breaks, product mix, and setup time. It is usually lower than design capacity.
How much of design capacity is actually being used. Utilization = Actual Output ÷ Design Capacity × 100.
How much of effective capacity is actually being achieved. Efficiency = Actual Output ÷ Effective Capacity × 100.
The slowest step or resource in a process that limits the output of the whole system. Improving non-bottleneck steps may not increase total output.
Anything that limits a system's ability to achieve more output or better performance. Constraints can be machines, labor, materials, policies, space, or demand.
A management approach focused on finding and improving the system's main constraint. The basic idea is that the whole process improves most when the bottleneck improves.
The effort to make a process faster, cheaper, safer, more reliable, or higher quality. It usually starts by measuring current performance and identifying waste or problems.
An activity that directly improves the product or service in a way the customer cares about. Managers try to protect and improve these activities.
An activity that uses time or resources but does not increase customer value. Examples include unnecessary waiting, movement, rework, and duplicate approvals.
A major redesign of a process rather than a small improvement. It asks what the process should look like if it were built from scratch.
A process type that produces low-volume, highly customized work. Examples include repair shops, custom furniture makers, and specialized medical clinics.
A process type that produces groups of similar items together. It offers more variety than repetitive production but usually has more setup time and waiting.
A process type that produces standardized products in high volume using a repeated sequence of steps. Assembly lines are a common example.
A process type that produces a very high volume of a standardized product with little interruption. Examples include oil refining, paper production, and chemical processing.
A process that delivers an activity, experience, or solution to a customer. Service processes often involve customer contact, waiting time, and variation in customer needs.

A framework that matches product variety and volume with the best process type. Low-volume custom products usually fit job shops, while high-volume standardized products fit repetitive or continuous processes.
A calculation used to find the volume where total revenue equals total cost. Break-Even Quantity = Fixed Costs ÷ (Price per Unit − Variable Cost per Unit).
The degree to which a product or service meets customer needs and expectations. In operations, quality means both doing the right things and doing them consistently.
How closely a product or service matches its design, specifications, or standards. A product with high conformance quality is made consistently with few defects.
How well a product or service performs in the eyes of the customer. It includes features, durability, service level, and overall usefulness.
A company-wide approach to continuously improving quality in every process and department. TQM emphasizes customer focus, employee involvement, prevention, and continuous improvement.
The process of checking finished work or work in progress to detect defects. It focuses on finding problems before products or services reach customers.
The process of designing systems to prevent defects from happening in the first place. It focuses on building quality into the process rather than inspecting it afterward.
Costs spent to avoid defects before they happen. Examples include training, process design, supplier certification, and preventive maintenance.
Costs spent to inspect, test, or measure quality. Examples include product inspections, audits, testing equipment, and quality reviews.
Costs from defects found before the product or service reaches the customer. Examples include scrap, rework, downtime, and retesting.
Costs from defects found after the product or service reaches the customer. Examples include returns, warranties, complaints, lawsuits, and lost reputation.
The total cost of preventing, detecting, and fixing quality problems. Cost of Quality = Prevention Costs + Appraisal Costs + Internal Failure Costs + External Failure Costs.
A data-driven method for reducing defects and process variation. Its goal is to make processes highly consistent and close to customer requirements.
A five-step Six Sigma improvement process: Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, and Control. It is used to solve process problems with data.
A measure of how many defects occur for every one million chances for defects. DPMO = (Number of Defects ÷ Number of Opportunities) × 1,000,000.
The use of data and charts to monitor whether a process is staying stable over time. It helps managers detect unusual changes before they become bigger problems.
A graph used to track process performance over time with a center line and control limits. It helps show whether variation looks normal or unusual.
The highest expected value for a stable process on a control chart. Points above this limit may signal a special problem or unusual cause.
The lowest expected value for a stable process on a control chart. Points below this limit may signal a special problem or unusual cause.
Natural variation that is built into a stable process. It is expected unless the process itself is changed.
Unusual variation caused by a specific event or problem outside the normal process. Examples include machine failure, bad materials, or an untrained worker.
A measure of whether a process can consistently produce output within customer or design limits. A capable process produces results that fit within the required range.

A measure of potential process capability assuming the process is centered between the specification limits. Cp = (Upper Specification Limit − Lower Specification Limit) ÷ (6 × Process Standard Deviation).
A measure of process capability that accounts for whether the process is centered. Cpk = Smaller of [(USL − Mean) ÷ (3σ), (Mean − LSL) ÷ (3σ)].
A set of international quality management standards. Certification signals that a company has documented processes for consistently meeting customer and regulatory requirements.
A U.S. award that recognizes organizations with excellent management and quality performance. It is often used as a model for evaluating overall organizational excellence.
A philosophy of continuous, small improvements involving employees at all levels. The goal is to keep improving processes instead of waiting for major redesigns.
A mistake-proofing method that prevents errors or makes them immediately obvious. Examples include connectors that only fit one way or forms that require missing fields to be completed.
The goal of doing work right the first time with no errors. In practice, it pushes managers to prevent defects rather than accept them as normal.
The needs, expectations, complaints, and preferences customers express or reveal. Operations teams use it to design products, services, and processes that actually fit customer priorities.
A planning method that translates customer needs into specific product or process features. It helps connect what customers want with what the company must design and control.
Goods or materials a company holds for future use or sale. Inventory can help meet demand, but it also ties up cash and creates storage risk.
The portion of inventory used to meet normal demand between orders. It exists because companies usually order or produce in batches rather than one unit at a time.
Extra inventory held to protect against uncertainty in demand or lead time. More safety stock reduces stockout risk but increases holding cost.
Inventory that has been ordered or produced but is still in transit or not yet available for use. It exists because moving goods through a supply chain takes time.
Demand for a finished product or item that comes directly from customers or the market. It must usually be forecasted because it is not calculated from another item's demand.
Demand for an item that depends on the demand for another item. For example, demand for tires depends on how many cars are being produced.
The order size that minimizes total annual ordering and holding costs. EOQ = √[(2DS) ÷ H], where D = annual demand, S = ordering cost per order, and H = annual holding cost per unit.
The cost of keeping inventory over time. It can include storage, insurance, spoilage, theft, damage, and the opportunity cost of cash tied up in inventory.
The cost of placing and receiving an inventory order. It can include purchasing paperwork, supplier communication, shipping setup, receiving, and inspection.
The cost of not having inventory available when it is needed. It can include lost sales, delays, unhappy customers, emergency orders, and damaged reputation.
The inventory level that triggers a new order. Reorder Point = Demand During Lead Time + Safety Stock.
The time between placing an order and receiving it. Longer or less predictable lead times usually require more inventory protection.
The amount of inventory expected to be used while waiting for a new order to arrive. Demand During Lead Time = Average Demand per Period × Lead Time.
The probability of having enough inventory to meet demand without a stockout during a replenishment cycle. Higher service levels usually require more safety stock.
The percentage of customer demand that is immediately filled from available inventory. Fill Rate = Demand Filled Immediately ÷ Total Demand × 100.
A measure of how many times inventory is sold or used during a period. Inventory Turnover = Cost of Goods Sold ÷ Average Inventory.
A measure of how many days inventory will last on average. Days of Inventory = 365 ÷ Inventory Turnover.
An inventory method that groups items by importance, usually based on annual dollar usage. Managers give the most attention to A items and less attention to lower-value C items.
The most important inventory items, often small in number but high in dollar value or business impact. These items need tight control, accurate records, and frequent review.
Inventory items with moderate value or importance. They receive more control than C items but less intensive attention than A items.
Inventory items with low value or low business impact. They are often controlled with simpler systems because close management may cost more than it saves.
An inventory approach that aims to receive materials only when they are needed for production or sale. It reduces inventory costs but requires reliable suppliers and stable processes.
A visual signal used to trigger production or replenishment. Kanban helps control inventory by pulling work forward only when it is needed.
An arrangement where the supplier monitors and replenishes the customer's inventory. It can reduce stockouts and improve coordination between buyer and supplier.
Inventory stored at the customer's location but still owned by the supplier until it is used or sold. This can reduce the buyer's inventory investment while keeping goods available.
An inventory system where stock levels are checked at fixed time intervals. Orders are placed after each review to bring inventory back up to a target level.
An inventory system where stock levels are monitored constantly or after every transaction. A new order is placed when inventory reaches the reorder point.
A simple inventory system that uses two containers of stock. When the first bin is empty, a reorder is triggered while the second bin covers demand during lead time.
An inventory system with a minimum level that triggers an order and a maximum level used as the target restock amount. It helps prevent both stockouts and excessive inventory.
